mechanism_narrative: A higher federal funds rate directly increases the discount rate applied to equity cash flows, mechanically lowering present values and producing negative S&P 500 forward returns over a 20-day horizon. This relationship is persistent across all rate environments.
failure_condition: Rank IC of FEDFUNDS level vs forward 20-day returns remains negative but with absolute magnitude < 0.02 over a 3-year rolling window, or a positive rank IC emerges.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp increase in the unemployment rate above a critical threshold of 4.5% signals an impending economic contraction, reducing corporate earnings expectations and leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the following 60 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:Historical recession triggers when unemployment rises 0.5pp above trough
failure_condition: In samples where UNRATE exceeds 4.5%, forward 60-day SPX returns are positive more than 55% of the time, or the average return is not statistically significantly negative.

mechanism_narrative: The joint product of inflation (CPIAUCSL level) and the fed funds rate (FEDFUNDS level) captures the aggregate monetary tightening burden on the economy: higher values indicate that both inflation and policy rates are elevated, squeezing corporate margins and equity valuations, producing negative forward returns over 20 days.
failure_condition: The product of CPIAUCSL and FEDFUNDS shows a positive rank IC with forward 20-day returns over a 2-year rolling window, or the decile spread fails to be monotonic negative.

mechanism_narrative: During a monetary tightening cycle defined by FEDFUNDS above 4% and rising, increases in industrial production (INDPRO) paradoxically signal an overheating economy that will prompt further rate hikes, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days.
regime_hint: monetary tightening cycle (FEDFUNDS > 4% and rising over 3 months)
failure_condition: In periods when FEDFUNDS > 4% and rising, INDPRO gains show a positive rank IC with forward 5-day returns, or the negative relationship is not statistically significant over two tightening cycles.

mechanism_narrative: Strong monthly growth in retail and food service sales (RSAFS) above 0.5% month-over-month signals robust consumer demand, driving up corporate revenues and earnings, which translates into positive S&P 500 returns over the subsequent 20 trading days.
failure_condition: When RSAFS monthly growth exceeds 0.5%, forward 20-day SPX returns are negative more than 55% of the time, or the average return is not significantly positive.

mechanism_narrative: Rising industrial production (INDPRO) reflects expanding manufacturing activity and supply capacity, which supports higher corporate profits and equity valuations, leading to positive S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days via a leading indicator effect.
failure_condition: The monthly change in INDPRO shows a negative rank IC with forward 5-day SPX returns over a 5-year rolling window, or the IC is indistinguishable from zero.

mechanism_narrative: The simultaneous improvement in both employment growth (PAYEMS increasing) and a decline in unemployment (UNRATE decreasing) captures a tightening labor market that boosts wage growth and consumer spending, creating a powerful positive impulse for equity returns over a 5-day horizon.
failure_condition: When both PAYEMS is rising and UNRATE is falling, forward 5-day SPX returns are negative or flat more than 50% of the time, or the combination does not improve predictive performance over either series alone.

mechanism_narrative: Strong quarterly GDP growth (GDPC1 annualized growth > 3%) indicates a booming aggregate demand that propels corporate earnings and investor optimism, resulting in positive S&P 500 returns over the subsequent 60 trading days as the data release confirms the expansion.
failure_condition: In periods where GDPC1 quarterly growth exceeds 3%, the average forward 60-day SPX return is not significantly positive (t-test p > 0.05), or the hit rate is below 55%.
